fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The process abstraction, the syscalls for working with processes on Unix, the hardware-software contract that makes time-sharing safe, and the scheduling policies that decide who runs when.
A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The minimum operations any OS must expose for working with Processes:

html:ol.html:li: Create: load a program from disk, set up its address space (stack, heap, code), and jump to main. The OS also initializes argc/argv.
Destroy: kill a misbehaving or unwanted process.
Wait: block until another process completes — useful for ordering dependent work.
Miscellaneous control: e.g. suspend / resume.
Status: report on running processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The stack is allocated up front. The heap starts small and grows via malloc, which calls into the OS (brk/sbrk) when it runs out of headroom.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: On Unix, the concrete realization of this API uses fork(), wait(), and exec() — covered separately.

The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
Remark. The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Blocks the calling Process until one of its children terminates. The OS marks the child's PCB ZOMBIE on exit, keeping enough state (exit status, resource usage) for the parent to retrieve via wait(); only when the parent collects it is the child fully reaped.
wait() returns the PID of the child that died. Variant waitpid(pid, ...) blocks on a specific child — useful when a parent has multiple children and only one is interesting.
Used to enforce ordering between parent and child code paths after fork(), or to know when an exec()'d program finishes.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling wait() with no children to wait on returns -1.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An asynchronous notification delivered to a Process. A process sends a signal with kill(pid, signum) (the name is misleading — most signals don't kill); the recipient can install a handler with signal(signum, handler) to "catch" it, otherwise the default action runs (often termination).

Signals are the OS's mechanism for events the recipient didn't ask to be told about: SIGINT from Ctrl-C, SIGSEGV from a Page fault on an invalid page, SIGCHLD when a child exits, SIGTERM from an administrative kill.
Who can signal whom depends on the user owning the process — a user can signal their own processes; the superuser can signal any process.

The OS lets user code run directly on the CPU for speed, but with hardware-enforced limits that prevent a buggy or malicious program from compromising the rest of the system. Two questions motivate the mechanism:

html:li.html:em: How do we make sure a program doesn't do what we don't want it to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first question is answered by hardware User mode vs kernel mode enforced via the Trap table, with a controlled Syscall mechanism as the only path from one to the other. The second is answered by periodic timer interrupts that trap to the OS, which performs a Context switch and runs the scheduler.
The whole stack is bootstrapped during the Boot sequence, when the kernel installs the trap table and starts the timer before handing control to user space.

Two privilege levels supported by the CPU. User mode restricts the running process to a limited set of instructions and a limited view of memory — usually just its own address space. Kernel mode runs the OS with full access to all instructions and all of physical memory.

Switching between modes is mediated by hardware via a Trap table populated at boot. The OS sets up the table; the CPU enforces it. A process cannot promote itself to kernel mode arbitrarily — it must invoke a Syscall mechanism, which traps to a fixed entry point in the OS.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Kernel mode is dangerous because the address space is effectively the entire machine. The mode bit + the trap table together are what makes it safe to time-share a CPU between mutually distrusting programs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A hardware-readable table installed by the OS at boot that maps each kind of exceptional control-flow event (syscall, interrupt, page fault, illegal instruction, ...) to the address of an OS handler. When the CPU takes a trap, it consults this table to decide where to jump, switches into kernel mode, and runs the handler.

The table is what makes it safe to expose privileged operations to user mode: user code never gets to pick the destination of a mode-switch, only the trap number. The OS controls every entry point, the CPU enforces the indirection.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Installing the trap table is itself a privileged operation. It happens once, early in boot, before any user process exists.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A syscall is a user-mode-initiated request for a privileged service from the kernel. Because user code cannot jump into kernel code directly, the mechanism is built around a single trap instruction plus a pre-agreed register convention identifying which service to perform.
The end-to-end sequence:

html:ol.html:li: User code places the syscall number in a pre-defined register and invokes the trap instruction.
Hardware saves enough state to resume the process and switches to kernel mode.
Hardware jumps to the OS-installed handler via the Trap table.
The handler reads the syscall number from the agreed register and dispatches to the corresponding OS routine.
The OS validates the request, performs the work, and arranges return values per the calling convention.
The OS restores user state and returns the process to user mode.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Because the register convention is fixed by ABI, the user-mode side of a syscall is typically hand-written in assembly within libc.
Remark. Interrupts work via a similar trap-handler mechanism, but are initiated by hardware (timer, device) rather than user code.
Remark. The kernel must validate any pointers or arguments the user supplies — user code cannot be trusted not to ask the kernel to read kernel memory on its behalf.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The operation of saving the full execution state of one process (or thread) and restoring another's so the CPU can resume it as if it had never been interrupted. On a trap into the kernel, the hardware stashes the user-visible state (PC, condition codes, stack pointer) into a known location, and the OS saves the remaining general-purpose registers as part of the switching code.
Two flavors of state are involved:

html:ol.html:li: User registers: the current program's context (general-purpose registers, stack vars).
Kernel registers: meta-information used to resume the trap correctly (PC at trap, syscall number, etc.).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Context switches are not free — they incur the cost of cache and TLB pollution in addition to the direct cost of saving/restoring state. This is the cost amortized by larger scheduling quanta and a recurring concern for any Concurrency system.
The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The scheduler decides which runnable Process to put on the CPU at each moment. There is no single right answer because the metrics conflict:

html:ol.html:li: Turnaround time: time from arrival to completion, T_{\text {completion}} - T_{\text {arrival}}. Minimized by running short jobs first.
Response time: time from arrival to first scheduling. Minimized by frequent rotation.
Fairness: every job makes progress; nobody starves.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First-in, first-out. Jobs are run to completion in the order they arrive. Trivially fair under any reasonable notion of fairness, but suffers from the convoy effect: a single long-running job at the head delays every shorter job behind it, inflating average turnaround time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. If the first arriving job is much longer than the rest, all subsequent jobs wait at least its full duration before getting any CPU.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shortest job first. Run the job with the smallest total work first, run-to-completion. If all jobs arrive at the same time, SJF provably minimizes average turnaround time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Real workloads don't all arrive at t = 0. A long job that arrives slightly before a short one still gets to monopolize the CPU until it finishes — SJF degenerates to FIFO scheduling under sequential arrivals.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Removing the run-to-completion constraint and allowing preemption gives STCF scheduling.

Shortest time-to-completion first. A preemptive refinement of SJF scheduling: whenever the scheduler is invoked, it picks the job with the least remaining work. A short job arriving mid-execution can preempt a long one.
STCF is provably optimal for minimizing average turnaround time over all preemptive schedulers.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Long jobs starve in response time. The lowest-priority work might not get a CPU slice for a very long time, which is unacceptable for interactive workloads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Cycle through all runnable jobs, giving each a fixed time slice — the stride, time slice, or quantum length — before preempting and moving to the next. The best you can do at minimizing response time: every job sees the CPU within at most N quanta where N is the runnable-job count.
The quantum length is a tradeoff:

html:ul.html:li: Short quantum: better response time, more frequent context switches, worse cache locality.
Long quantum: amortizes context-switch cost, worse response time.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worst-case response time for N jobs at quantum S is S(N-1).
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Round-robin minimizes response time but is poor for turnaround time relative to STCF scheduling. A scheduler that wants both has to combine ideas — see Multi-level feedback queue.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A practical scheduler that approximates STCF scheduling's turnaround properties and Round-robin scheduling's response properties without requiring foreknowledge of job lengths. Maintain multiple priority queues; within a queue, schedule round-robin. Never service a lower-priority queue while a higher-priority queue has a runnable job.
The priority of a job is not assigned by the user — it is learned from behavior:

html:ul.html:li: New jobs enter at the top priority.
Each priority level has an allotment — the total CPU time a job may consume before being demoted.
Once a job exhausts its allotment at a level, it drops to the next level.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Short jobs that finish quickly never leave the top queue (good turnaround). Interactive jobs that yield often stay at the top (good response time). CPU-bound jobs sink to the bottom and get scheduled when nothing better is runnable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Gaming the allotment: a job can issue I/O just before its allotment expires to avoid demotion, since I/O time doesn't count.
Solution. Charge the job's allotment for total time at the priority level, including time spent blocked.
Problem. Starvation of low-priority queues if higher queues never empty.
Solution. Periodically reset all jobs to the top priority. Cheap and effective.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A proportional-share scheduler. Each runnable job holds some number of tickets, drawn from a fixed total. On each scheduling decision, the scheduler picks a random ticket and runs the job that owns it. Over time, a job receives CPU time proportional to its share of the tickets.
The implementation is trivial: one random number per decision, plus a sum table. No global state about historical CPU usage is needed.
Mechanisms layered on top:

html:ul.html:li: Ticket currency — give each user a fixed number of tickets to split among their processes however they like.
Ticket transfer — a client process can hand tickets to a server to boost its priority while servicing the request.
Ticket inflation — a process can grow its share temporarily, with bounds set by trust.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The unsolved problem is how to assign tickets in the first place. The mechanism is simple; the policy is not.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: A deterministic version of the same idea is Stride scheduling, which avoids the variance of random sampling at the cost of needing global state.

A deterministic proportional-share scheduler. Each job has a stride inversely proportional to its ticket count (the more tickets, the smaller the stride) and a running pass counter that accumulates its stride every time it's scheduled.
The decision is:

html:ol.html:li: Pick the job with the lowest pass.
Run it for one quantum.
Increment its pass by its stride.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A job with twice as many tickets has half the stride, so its pass grows half as fast, so it gets scheduled twice as often. Same long-run behavior as Lottery scheduling, but with zero variance.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. New jobs need an initial pass value. Setting it to 0 lets them dominate; setting it to the global minimum lets them merge in cleanly but requires tracking that minimum.
Remark. Each context switch is not free. Choosing a longer time slice amortizes that cost at the price of response time.
Remark. I/O-bound jobs are commonly handled by splitting a job into its non-I/O segments and scheduling them individually — turning one long-but-mostly-blocked job into many short, runnable ones.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Blocks the calling Process until one of its children terminates. The OS marks the child's PCB ZOMBIE on exit, keeping enough state (exit status, resource usage) for the parent to retrieve via wait(); only when the parent collects it is the child fully reaped.
wait() returns the PID of the child that died. Variant waitpid(pid, ...) blocks on a specific child — useful when a parent has multiple children and only one is interesting.
Used to enforce ordering between parent and child code paths after fork(), or to know when an exec()'d program finishes.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling wait() with no children to wait on returns -1.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worked homework for Three Easy Pieces. One section per chapter; the conceptual material for each chapter lives in the corresponding concept pages — Processes Intro across Process, Process API, Process control block; Process Interlude across fork(), wait(), exec(), Unix signals.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads does SJF scheduling deliver the same turnaround as FIFO scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For workloads that arrive in increasing order of total job time.
html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads and quantum lengths does SJF deliver the same response times as Round-robin scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For shorter jobs and longer quantum lengths, RR and SJF should deliver similar response times.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with SJF as job lengths increase?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Response time degrades because the longest jobs have to wait for each of the shorter jobs to finish. If these shorter jobs become longer than the response time grows linearly.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with RR as quantum lengths increase? Can you write an equation that gives the worst-case response time, given N jobs?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. The response time is the average over stride length number of preceding jobs. The formula can be written as \mathrm {RT} = \frac {1}{N} \sum _{i = 1}^{N} (i - 1)S = \frac {S(N - 1) N}{N} = S(N - 1)
html:ol.html:li:
Run process.py with the flags -l 5:100, 5:100. What should the CPU utilization be?
Answer. The flags correspond to running two processes each with 5 instructions where each instruction has probability one of using the CPU. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu with this, we can conclude that the utilization is 100%. We will execute one instruction for the first process until it completes, then the five instructions of the next process. If both are waiting on IO, then I do not think this would be the case?
Solution. Using the solution flags -cp, there is 100% utilization.

Run process.py with the flags -l 4:100, 1:0. How long does it take to complete both processes?
Answer. The flags correspond to running one process with 4 instructions with each instruction having probability one of using the CPU and one process with one instruction that uses the IO. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 io io_done It will take 4 + \text {time of IO}. Since the process with 4 non-IO-dependent instructions will run each of those instructions and then the next process will begin which must wait for the entire duration of IO.
Solution. Using the -cp flags it takes 11 timesteps to complete which is \approx 4 + \text {time of IO}.

Now switch the order of the processes: ./process-run.py -l 1:0,4:100. What happens now? Does switching the order mat- ter? Why?
Answer. The output of the code is Process 0 io io_done
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu Depending on the time of IO (the -L flag), it should take \max (5, 1 + \text {time IO}). Switching the order does matter because we can run the CPU instructions while waiting for non-blocking IO. This motivates the idea of scheduling.
Solution. It only takes 7 timesteps because we are able to perform the IO operations off the CPU as expected.

We'll now explore some of the other flags. One important flag is -S, which determines how the system reacts when a process issues an I/O. With the flag set to SWITCH_ON_END, the system will NOT switch to another process while one is doing I/O, instead waiting until the process is completely finished. What happens when you run the following two processes, one doing I/O and the other doing CPU work? (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH_ON_END)
Answer. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED READY 1 3 BLOCKED READY 1 4 BLOCKED READY 1 5 BLOCKED READY 1 6 BLOCKED READY 1 7* RUN:io_done READY 1 8 DONE RUN:cpu 1 9 DONE RUN:cpu 1 10 DONE RUN:cpu 1 11 DONE RUN:cpu 1 As expected, it runs the IO process, blocking the CPU process until it is completed.

Now, run the same processes, but with the switching behavior set to switch to another process whenever one is WAITING for I/O (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH ON IO). What happens now? Use -c and -p to confirm that you are right
html:ol.html:div: Answer. I assume that at timestep 1 we will still RUN:io. However, we will then switch the PID: 1 for the next 4 timesteps until it is completed. After one additional BLOCKED timestep, we will run RUN:io_done and complete both processes.

Solution. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 3 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 4 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 5 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 6 BLOCKED DONE 1 7* RUN:io_done DONE 1
html:ol.html:li:
One other important behavior is what to do when an I/O completes. With -I IO RUN LATER, when an I/O completes, the process that issued it is not necessarily run right away; rather, whatever was running at the time keeps running. What happens when you run this combination of processes? (./process-run.py -l 3:0,5:100,5:100,5:100 -S SWITCH ON IO -I IO RUN LATER -c -p) Are system resources being effectively utilized?
Answer. The resources are not efficiently utilized because we end up waiting until all of our CPU-bound processes finish before returning to the IO-bound process, which calls another IO:run and we do not get any concurrency benefit.
Solution. The code output is pretty long but the end output is Stats: Total Time 31 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (67.74%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (48.39%)

Now run the same processes, but with -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE set, which immediately runs the process that issued the I/O. How does this behavior differ? Why might running a process that just completed an I/O again be a good idea?
Answer. The behavior differs by returning to the IO-bound process, which can then immediately call the next IO:run before returning access to the CPU-bound processes, improving concurrency. In an attempt to predict the next IO call, it might be a good idea to run the process that just completed IO because it is more likely to make another IO call.
Solution. The corresponding statistics are Stats: Total Time 21 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (100.00%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (71.43%)

Now run with some randomly generated processes, e.g., -s 1 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 2 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 3 -l 3:50,3:50. See if you can predict how the trace will turn out. What happens when you use -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE vs. -I IO RUN LATER? What hap- pens when you use -S SWITCH ON IO vs. -S SWITCH ON END?
Answer. Since each instruction has a 50% chance of being CPU or I/O, I do not think there will be much benefit to IO_RUN_LATER vs. IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE. However, I believe it will be crucial to enable -S SWITCH_ON_IO over -S SWITCH_ON_END as the number of processes grow because we should always have waiting CPU instructions to run. In the case of two processes, we still may not improve that much because they will likely both be IO-bound at some point.
Solution. For -s 1 (aka the seed), the observed performance between IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE and IO_RUN_LATER is the same. However, we do observe an improvement on both IO and CPU usage when we enable SWITCH_ON_IO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (6/66).

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork(). Before calling fork(), have the main process access a variable (e.g., x) and set its value to something (e.g., 100). What value is the variable in the child process? What happens to the variable when both the child and parent change the value of x
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both the parent and the child get a copy of the variable values i.e. x. When they modify x it only affects the copy of x in their local address space.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that opens a file (with the open() system call) and then calls fork() to create a new process. Can both the child and parent access the file descriptor returned by open()? What happens when they are writing to the file concurrently, i.e., at the same time?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both do have access to the same file descriptor. I believe that they can interleave writes if writing at the same time but for me whichever process got ran first always finished writing prior the other starting. This may be a scheduling thing, where one process is able to complete before the other is scheduled?
html:ol.html:li: Write another program using fork(). The child process should print "hello"; the parent process should print "goodbye". You should try to ensure that the child process always prints first; can you do this without calling wait() in the parent?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Using the previous problem, in the child, we can write something to a file. Then, in the parent, we can enforce an ordering by making the parent wait in a while loop until it reads the value written by the child. However, this is terribly inconvenient (and probably why wait() exists).
html:ol.html:li: Now write a program that uses wait() to wait for the child process to finish in the parent. What does wait() return? What happens if you use wait() in the child?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. wait() returns the pid of the child process that died. Using wait() in the child just immediately returns -1.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork() and then calls some form of exec() to run the program /bin/ls. See if you can try all of the variants of exec(), including execl(), execle(), execlp(), execv(), execvp(), and execvP(). Why do you think there are so many variants of the same basic call?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. It seems that some of them support different types of arguments i.e. execv family allows variable size argument list. Additionally, there is a distinction in the environment. This seems to be a list of environment variable values that exec{l,v}e() will search over. The p family use the same search as the shell i.e. the PATH.
html:ol.html:li: Write a slight modification of the previous program, this time using waitpid() instead of wait(). When would waitpid() be useful?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. If a parent process has multiple children, then wait() will only wait until the first one dies. waitpid() lets you specify waiting on a specific child process.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates a child process, and then in the child closes standard output (STDOUT_FILENO). What happens if the child calls printf() to print some output after closing the descriptor?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Nothing prints in the child process. However, stdout is still open in the parent process and able to print.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates two children, and connects the standard output of one to the standard input of the other, using the pipe() system call
html:ol.html:div: Answer. This required fflush() for me. I am not sure if this is true in general?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An optimization where two address spaces share the same physical page until one writes to it. Both processes have PTEs pointing at the same PPN, but with the write bit cleared. The first write traps to the OS, which allocates a fresh page, copies the contents, points the writing process's PTE at the new page (with writes re-enabled), and resumes.
Used pervasively:

html:ul.html:li: fork() — the child shares the parent's pages until either modifies one. Avoids the cost of copying an entire address space the child often immediately overwrites with exec().
Shared libraries — many processes map the same library text pages.
Kernel mapped into every process — same physical kernel code seen from every address space.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.
